title: The Russian-Chechen Conflict and the Putin-Kadyrov Connection
strengths: Clear summary of the transition from open warfare to insurgency and of the later federal-to-local devolution of counterinsurgency authority.
limitations: Not a primary casualty database; broad historical synthesis rather than a named-victim or deployment register.
notes: Used mainly for episode boundaries, mode shifts, and quality-control on military-loss discussions.

title: Are Coethnics More Effective Counterinsurgents? Evidence from the Second Chechen War
author_or_org: Jason Lyall / American Political Science Review
strengths: Peer-reviewed; explicitly distinguishes fixed-battle phase from guerrilla phase and gives useful insurgent-strength context for the open-war denominator.
limitations: Primary focus is counterinsurgency effectiveness, not complete mortality accounting.
notes: Used to justify the split between fixed-battle and insurgency phases and to bound insurgent presence in the military denominator.

title: Massacres of Civilians in Chechnya
author_or_org: Anne le Huérou; Amandine Regamey / Mass Violence & Résistance (Sciences Po)
geographic_scope: Chechnya, especially Grozny and massacre/bombardment sites
date_scope: Mainly late 1999 to 2000 with later disappearances context
strengths: Best accessible synthesis tying together HRW, Memorial, ECHR, and other documentation; provides both aggregate and incident-level civilian fatality evidence.
limitations: Secondary synthesis; some underlying sources are cited but not directly reproduced. Aggregate civilian estimate is not side-allocated.
notes: Core civilian numerator source for the open-war episode.

title: Civilian Killings in Staropromyslovski District of Grozny
strengths: Victim- and witness-based incident report with explicit attacker identification.
limitations: Incident-specific, not republic-wide; minimum count only.
notes: Used to anchor side-attributed civilian killings in the open-war phase.

title: More than sixty civilians murdered in Chechen capital (Press Release, 2/23/2000)
strengths: Explicit distinction between identified victims and higher body count observed on site.
notes: Useful for bracketing a massacre count and showing within-source low/high logic.

title: Russian Federation: What Justice for Chechnya's Disappeared?
strengths: Explicitly separates the killed from the missing and gives a whole-war civilian ceiling plus disappearance range.
limitations: Not episode-specific; 'up to 25,000' is an upper-bound summary rather than a phase-specific series.
notes: Used to keep disappearances separate from direct-death DR and to contextualize later-war civilian harm.

title: Russian Air Power over Chechnya: Lessons Learned Applied
pages_or_sections: p.12; search-result snippet and PDF screenshot
strengths: Gives clear force-size benchmarks for the Joint Grouping of Forces across the war's early years.
limitations: Not a deployment-by-month register; focuses on military organization and airpower, not casualties.
notes: Used for the upper and baseline federal-force presence in the open-war denominator.

title: Chechnya: Rebels Give Count Of Losses
strengths: Contemporaneous report that places official Russian losses and rebel self-reported losses in the same item.
limitations: Neither side's figures are independently verified; article itself warns numbers are generally believed to be higher.
notes: Used to build the conservative lower bound for combatant deaths in the open-war episode.

title: Chechnya in Figures: Survey of the Population of the Republic of Chechnya
author_or_org: Danish Refugee Council / Danish People's Aid (ASF) via ReliefWeb
strengths: Direct humanitarian-survey wording on March 2000 in-republic population estimates.
limitations: Only the search-result snippet was accessible in this environment; full report could not be fetched because of site restrictions.
notes: Used as the lower anchor for civilian presence during the siege phase.

title: Plight of Refugees and Civilians in Chechnya Proper
strengths: Accessible quotation from DRC/ASF report no. 28 with date and district coverage.
limitations: Secondary summary of the underlying humanitarian report.
notes: Used to bound the upper civilian-presence scenario and to document district coverage of the registration exercise.

title: North Caucasus Emergency Health Update No. 17
strengths: Provides the key warning that the 733,889 registration figure includes roughly 47,000 dual-registered IDPs.
limitations: Only the search-result snippet was accessible in this environment.
notes: Used to avoid overcounting civilians in the denominator ceiling.

title: Ingushetia Situation Report No. 14
strengths: Contemporaneous humanitarian tally of registered IDPs from Chechnya in Ingushetia.
limitations: Only the search-result snippet was accessible in this environment.
notes: Used to anchor the scale of civilians absent from Chechnya during the siege phase.

title: Chechnya (Written Answer, Commons, 28 April 2003)
strengths: Accessible quotation of Danish Refugee Council estimates and simultaneous official population claim.
limitations: Not an original survey document; later-period rather than open-war measurement.
notes: Used to show why later insurgency denominators must be treated differently and why official population claims need critical handling.

title: DZHABRAILOV: 160,000 People Killed in Two Chechen Wars
strengths: Useful for showing how political totals diverge from narrower HRW/Memorial estimates and why they should not be averaged mechanically.
limitations: Secondary article discussing highly politicized claims; not suitable as a direct estimate path.
notes: Used as a disagreement check, not as a core numerator source.

title: Casualty Figures
strengths: Explicitly documents contradictory Russian official military-loss statements and Soldiers' Mothers' far higher estimate.
limitations: Later than the open-war phase and not episode-specific enough for direct use in the main estimate.
notes: Used to justify conservative handling of official military loss figures and the wide uncertainty band.

title: North Caucasus Situation Report No. 40
limitations: Only the search-result snippet was accessible in this environment.
notes: Supports the claim that post-siege civilian presence recovered enough to make a single 1999-2009 denominator invalid.
